topics.title: Pass by Value in Java
topics.description: Java is strictly pass-by-value for all types — primitives and object references alike. When you pass a primitive, you pass a copy of its value. When you pass an object, you pass a copy of the reference (pointer), not the object itself. Mutations through the copied reference affect the real object, but reassigning the reference inside a method never affects the caller's variable.
topics.story: Imagine you own a house. You photocopy your house key and hand the copy to a friend. Your friend can use that copy to enter your house and rearrange the furniture (mutate the object). But if your friend melts that key copy and forges a new one for a different house, your original key still opens your original house. That's pass-by-value for references — the copy can affect what it points to, but it can't change what you're pointing at.
topics.analogy: Think of a variable as a GPS coordinate. Passing a primitive is like writing the coordinates on a sticky note and handing the note over — the recipient can write new coordinates on their note, yours stays unchanged. Passing an object reference is like giving someone a printed copy of your GPS coordinates — they can drive to that location and renovate the place, but if they erase the coordinates on their paper and write a new address, your copy still says the old address.
topics.memoryAnchor: Java = Pass by VALUE always. For objects: you pass a copy of the reference (address), not the object. Mutations = real (affect the object at the address). Reassignment = fake (only changes the local copy of the address). Mnemonic: 'Copy of the Map, not the Territory'.
topics.firstPrinciples: All local variables (including parameters) live on the stack frame. When you call a method, Java copies the value of the argument into the parameter slot of the new stack frame. For primitives, that value IS the data. For objects, that value is the memory address (reference). So you can mutate through the address but you can't change which address the caller holds.
topics.bruteForce: If Java were truly pass-by-reference (like C++ references), reassigning a parameter inside a method would change the caller's variable. This would make it impossible to reason about code locally — any method call could silently swap out your objects. Java chose pass-by-value for safety and simplicity.
topics.optimizedApproach: To return modified data, either: (1) return the new value, (2) use a mutable object (array, StringBuilder, custom wrapper) and mutate through the reference, (3) use an AtomicReference/single-element array as a workaround for lambda contexts. Prefer (1) — functional style. Use (2) only when mutation is semantically correct.
topics.whenToUse: This concept matters when: debugging why a swap(a, b) method doesn't work, understanding why StringBuilder mutation works but String reassignment doesn't, analyzing why passing an array and modifying its elements works but reassigning the array doesn't, or designing APIs that need to return multiple values (use return types / wrappers rather than output parameters).

examples.title: Primitive Pass by Value — Swap Fails
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"int x = 10, y = 20;","variables":{"x":10,"y":20},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"x and y are primitives stored directly in main()'s stack frame"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"swap(x, y);","variables":{"x":10,"y":20},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Java copies the VALUES 10 and 20 into swap()'s stack frame as parameters a and b"},{"line":3,"lineCode":"static void swap(int a, int b) {","variables":{"a":10,"b":20,"x":10,"y":20},"phase":"ENTER","annotation":"New stack frame: a=10, b=20. These are INDEPENDENT copies of x and y"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int temp = a;","variables":{"a":10,"b":20,"temp":10},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"temp gets copy of a's value"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"a = b;","variables":{"a":20,"b":20,"temp":10},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"a changed to 20 — but x in main() is still 10"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"b = temp;","variables":{"a":20,"b":10,"temp":10},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"b changed to 10 — but y in main() is still 20"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"System.out.println(\"After : x=\" + x + \", y=\" + y);","variables":{"x":10,"y":20},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"swap()'s frame was popped. x and y were never touched — they lived in main()'s frame the whole time"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph main_frame[main() Stack Frame]
      X[x = 10]
      Y[y = 20]
    end
    subgraph swap_frame[swap() Stack Frame - copy]
      A[a = 10 → 20]
      B[b = 20 → 10]
      T[temp = 10]
    end
    X -->|copy of value 10| A
    Y -->|copy of value 20| B
    X -.->|unchanged| X2[x still = 10]
    Y -.->|unchanged| Y2[y still = 20]
examples.pseudocode:
CALL swap(x, y):
  new stack frame: a = copy(x), b = copy(y)
  swap a and b locally
  frame popped — changes die with frame
  x, y in main() frame: untouched
examples.explanation: The swap method successfully swaps its LOCAL copies a and b, but those are independent variables in swap()'s stack frame. When swap() returns, that frame is gone. x and y in main() were never modified. This is why Java doesn't have a built-in swap for primitives — you'd need to return both values.
examples.realWorldUse: This is one of the most common Java interview gotchas. In production code, if you need to swap two values in-place, use an array index swap: arr[i] ^= arr[j]; arr[j] ^= arr[i]; arr[i] ^= arr[j]; (XOR swap). Or simply: int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; Arrays work because you're mutating through a shared reference.

examples.title: Object Reference — Mutation Works, Reassignment Doesn't
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":14,"lineCode":"List<String> myList = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(\"a\", \"b\"));","variables":{"myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"myList holds a reference (address 0x100) to the ArrayList on the heap"},{"line":16,"lineCode":"addElement(myList, \"c\");","variables":{"myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Java copies the reference value 0x100 into addElement's 'list' parameter"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"list.add(item);","variables":{"list":"ref→Heap@0x100","item":"c"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"list.add() navigates to address 0x100 and calls add on the SAME ArrayList. Real mutation!"},{"line":17,"lineCode":"System.out.println(myList);","variables":{"myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"myList still points to 0x100. The object at 0x100 now has [a, b, c] — mutation was real"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"replaceList(myList);","variables":{"myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Again, copies reference 0x100 into replaceList's 'list' parameter"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"list = new ArrayList<>();","variables":{"list":"ref→Heap@0x200","myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"REASSIGN","annotation":"new ArrayList() creates object at 0x200. Local 'list' now points to 0x200. myList STILL points to 0x100"},{"line":20,"lineCode":"System.out.println(\"After replaceList: \" + myList);","variables":{"myList":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"replaceList() returned. Its local 'list' copy died. myList unchanged, still [a, b, c]"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph main_frame
      ML[myList = 0x100]
    end
    subgraph addElement_frame
      L1[list = 0x100 copy]
    end
    subgraph replaceList_frame
      L2[list = 0x100 → 0x200]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      H1[0x100: ArrayList a,b,c]
      H2[0x200: new ArrayList]
    end
    ML -->|same address| H1
    L1 -->|same address - mutates| H1
    L2 -.->|local reassign only| H2
    ML -.->|never redirected| H1
examples.pseudocode:
PASS object reference:
  param = copy of reference (same address)
  obj.mutate() → affects real object (same address)
  param = new Object() → only changes local copy
  caller's reference unchanged
examples.explanation: addElement receives a copy of the reference pointing to the same ArrayList. Calling .add() on it goes to the real object — mutation is visible to the caller. replaceList also receives a copy, but when it does list = new ArrayList(), it redirects its LOCAL copy to a new object. The caller's myList still points to the original. Understanding this split — mutation vs reassignment — is fundamental to Java API design.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring beans are passed as references throughout the framework. Dependency injection gives you a reference to a singleton bean — you can call methods on it (mutation works), but if you assign bean = new MyService() in some method, you only change your local variable, not the Spring-managed instance. Same principle applies to Android Activity context passing.

examples.title: String Immutability + Pass by Value
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":13,"lineCode":"String str = \"Hello\";","variables":{"str":"ref→Pool@0xA00"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"str references the String \"Hello\" in String Pool"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"tryToModify(str);","variables":{"str":"ref→Pool@0xA00"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Copies reference 0xA00 into tryToModify's 's' parameter"},{"line":3,"lineCode":"s = s + \" World\";","variables":{"s":"ref→Heap@0xB00","str":"ref→Pool@0xA00"},"phase":"REASSIGN","annotation":"String + creates a NEW String \"Hello World\" at 0xB00. Local 's' reassigned to 0xB00. str still at 0xA00"},{"line":17,"lineCode":"StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(\"Hello\");","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xC00"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"sb references a mutable StringBuilder at 0xC00"},{"line":18,"lineCode":"tryToModifySB(sb);","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xC00"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Copies reference 0xC00 into tryToModifySB's 'sb' parameter"},{"line":8,"lineCode":"sb.append(\" World\");","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xC00"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"append() called on the SAME object at 0xC00. StringBuilder IS mutable — internal char[] modified in place"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"System.out.println(\"After tryToModifySB: \" + sb);","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xC00"},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"sb still points to 0xC00. Object at 0xC00 now contains \"Hello World\" — mutation visible to caller"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph String_immutable
      S1[str → 'Hello' in Pool]
      S2[s copy → 'Hello']
      S3[s after concat → 'Hello World' NEW obj]
      S1 -.->|unchanged| S1
    end
    subgraph StringBuilder_mutable
      SB1[sb → StringBuilder@0xC00]
      SB2[param sb copy → same 0xC00]
      SB3[append mutates 0xC00 in place]
    end
    SB2 --> SB3
examples.pseudocode:
String: immutable — every operation creates new object
  s = s + " World"  →  new String object, local reassign
  original unchanged

StringBuilder: mutable — operations modify internal array
  sb.append()  →  same object modified
  change visible through all references to it
examples.explanation: Two forces at play: (1) pass-by-value copies the reference, and (2) String immutability means every operation creates a new object. Even if Java were pass-by-reference, 's = s + World' would create a new String. The combination means String parameters can never be modified through a method call. StringBuilder is mutable, so append() modifies the shared object — the change IS visible to the caller.
examples.realWorldUse: This is why Java utility methods that 'build' a string take StringBuilder as a parameter, not String. For example, recursive tree printing methods accept a StringBuilder and append to it level-by-level. In Apache Commons Lang and Guava, many string utilities return new Strings rather than modifying inputs — because they can't.

examples.title: Array Parameter Mutation
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":23,"lineCode":"int[] nums = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};","variables":{"nums":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"nums holds address 0x500 of int[] on heap"},{"line":25,"lineCode":"doubleElements(nums);","variables":{"nums":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Copies reference 0x500 into doubleElements' 'arr' parameter"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"arr[i] *= 2;","variables":{"arr":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"arr[i] navigates to address 0x500, then to element i. Modifies the actual heap memory"},{"line":28,"lineCode":"replaceArray(nums);","variables":{"nums":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Copies reference 0x500 into replaceArray's 'arr'"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"arr = new int[]{100, 200, 300};","variables":{"arr":"ref→Heap@0x600","nums":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"REASSIGN","annotation":"new int[] allocates at 0x600. Local 'arr' now points there. nums STILL at 0x500"},{"line":29,"lineCode":"System.out.println(Arrays.toString(nums));","variables":{"nums":"ref→Heap@0x500"},"phase":"PRINT","annotation":"nums unchanged. replaceArray created a new array orphan that will be GC'd"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    N[nums = 0x500]
    subgraph Heap
      H1[0x500: int[] 1,2,3,4,5]
      H2[0x600: int[] 100,200,300 - orphan]
    end
    N -->|doubleElements: same address, mutate elements| H1
    N -.->|replaceArray: local copy redirected to 0x600, nums untouched| H1
examples.pseudocode:
ARRAY mutation (works):
  arr[i] = val  →  navigate to heap address, modify element
  caller sees change (same address)

ARRAY reassignment (fails):
  arr = new int[]  →  local copy redirected
  caller's reference unchanged

BEST PRACTICE: return new array
examples.explanation: Arrays are objects in Java. The reference to an array is passed by value. Modifying arr[i] uses that reference to reach and modify the heap element — visible to caller. But arr = new int[]{...} only redirects the local copy. The cleanest pattern for methods that produce a new array is to return it explicitly, as createDoubled() does.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Arrays.sort() sorts in-place by mutating through the reference — that's why it returns void. Collections.sort() does the same for List. If you want a sorted copy, you must copy first: int[] sorted = Arrays.copyOf(arr, arr.length); Arrays.sort(sorted); This pattern is everywhere in competitive programming and production Java.

examples.title: Workaround: Returning Multiple Values via Wrapper
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":23,"lineCode":"int[] q = new int[1], r = new int[1];","variables":{"q":"ref→Heap[0]","r":"ref→Heap[0]"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Single-element arrays act as mutable containers for primitive output"},{"line":24,"lineCode":"divideAndRemainder(17, 5, q, r);","variables":{"q":"ref→Heap[0]","r":"ref→Heap[0]"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Pass references to q and r arrays — method can mutate q[0] and r[0]"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"quotient[0] = a / b;","variables":{"quotient":"ref→same q","a":17,"b":5},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"quotient[0] = 3. Mutates through the reference — q[0] in caller is now 3"},{"line":28,"lineCode":"DivResult res = divideAndRemainderOOP(17, 5);","variables":{"res":{"quotient":3,"remainder":2}},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Pattern 2: clean OOP solution — return a record with both values"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Need multiple output values] --> B{Strategy}
    B -->|Primitive workaround| C[Single-element array int[1]]
    B -->|Clean OOP| D[Return record/class]
    B -->|Competitive programming| E[Return int[] array]
    C -->|array element mutation visible to caller| F[Works]
    D -->|new object returned| F
    E -->|new array returned| F
examples.pseudocode:
PROBLEM: Java can't return primitives by reference
SOLUTION A: wrap in single-element array (hacky but works)
SOLUTION B: create result record/class (idiomatic Java)
SOLUTION C: return int[] (common in algorithms)
SOLUTION D: use AtomicInteger (for lambdas/anonymous classes)
examples.explanation: Since Java can't pass primitives by reference, returning multiple values requires explicit packaging. The record pattern (Java 14+) is the cleanest: creates an immutable value object inline. For legacy code, a simple int[] return works. Single-element array output parameters exist in old Java code (like Scanner internals) but are considered a code smell today.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's standard library itself uses this: Math.divideUnsigned() and Math.floorDivMod() return single values. For quotient+remainder together, use Math.floorDivMod(a, b) (Java 18+) which returns a 2-element int[]. In competitive programming, returning int[] for multiple values is standard: int[] result = {lo, hi}; return result;

problems.title: Predict the Output — Primitive Swap
problems.hint1: Inside swap(), a and b are local copies — swapping them doesn't affect x and y.
problems.hint2: The first println is inside swap() — it prints the swapped local values.
problems.hint3: The second println is in main() — x and y were never changed.
problems.editorial: swap()'s local copies a and b are swapped (prints 10 5). But x and y in main() are primitives in a separate stack frame — never touched. They print 5 10 unchanged.

problems.title: Working Swap Using Array
problems.description: Implement `swapInArray(int[] arr, int i, int j)` that swaps elements at indices i and j. This works because you're mutating through a reference, not trying to swap primitive variables.
problems.hint1: Access arr[i] and arr[j] — both go through the reference to the same heap array.
problems.hint2: Use a temp variable: int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp;
problems.hint3: No return value needed — the caller's array is modified in place.
problems.editorial: Array elements are on the heap; arr is a reference. arr[i] navigates to the real heap element and modifies it. The caller's array variable still points to the same array — it sees the mutation immediately.

problems.title: Does This Modify the Original? — String
problems.description:
Will calling `appendSuffix(str, " World")` modify the original String variable? Implement `doesModify()` that returns false if the original is unchanged, or implement `appendSuffix` correctly to actually change what the caller sees (hint: you can't — return the new value instead).

Implement `String appendSuffix(String s, String suffix)` that returns the concatenated result.
problems.hint1: String is immutable — s + suffix creates a new String object.
problems.hint2: The local parameter s is reassigned inside the method, but the caller's variable is unchanged.
problems.hint3: Return the new concatenated String instead of trying to modify the parameter.
problems.editorial: Strings are immutable + passed by value. Even if you wrote s = s + suffix inside the method, that reassigns the local copy only. Returning the new String is the correct Java idiom.

problems.title: Increment Counter Via Wrapper
problems.description: Java can't increment a primitive through a method call. Implement `increment(int[] counter)` that increments counter[0] by 1. Then implement the same using AtomicInteger. Demonstrate both work for a caller that needs the incremented value.
problems.hint1: Use int[] counter = {0}; then pass it to increment(). The method modifies counter[0].
problems.hint2: AtomicInteger wraps an int in a mutable object with incrementAndGet().
problems.hint3: Both approaches work because you're mutating through an object reference.
problems.editorial: counter[0]++ navigates through the array reference to the heap element and mutates it — visible to caller. AtomicInteger is a mutable wrapper around int with thread-safe operations. Both work because you're mutating an object, not trying to reassign a primitive parameter.

problems.title: Trace Reference Aliasing
problems.hint1: b = a makes b and a point to the same ArrayList object.
problems.hint2: b.add(4) mutates the SAME object — a.size() sees it.
problems.hint3: new ArrayList<>(a) creates a COPY — c and a are independent. c.add(5) doesn't affect a.
problems.editorial: b=a creates aliasing: both point to the same heap object. b.add(4) modifies that object — a sees size 4. new ArrayList<>(a) creates a defensive copy — c and a are now independent objects. c.add(5) only changes c.

problems.title: Reverse Array In-Place
problems.description: Implement `reverse(int[] arr)` that reverses the array in-place. Since arrays are passed by reference copy, mutations to arr[i] are visible to the caller. Verify by printing the original array after calling reverse.
problems.hint1: Use two pointers: left=0, right=arr.length-1.
problems.hint2: Swap arr[left] and arr[right], then move both pointers inward.
problems.hint3: Stop when left >= right.
problems.editorial: Two-pointer approach swaps from outside in. O(n/2) swaps, O(1) space. Works in-place because arr[i] mutations go through the reference to the real heap array.

problems.title: Return Modified String (Builder Pattern)
problems.description: Implement `buildGreeting(String name, String title, int year)` that constructs and returns: `'Dear Dr. {name}, Welcome to class of {year}!'`. Use StringBuilder internally. Demonstrate that String parameters are not modified.
problems.hint1: Create a StringBuilder inside the method and append to it.
problems.hint2: Concatenating with + inside a loop is O(n²) — StringBuilder is O(n).
problems.hint3: Return sb.toString() at the end.

testCases.output: Dear Dr. Smith, Welcome to class of 2024!
Dear Prof. Johnson, Welcome to class of 2025!

problems.constraints: name and title are non-null, non-empty. year > 0.
problems.editorial: StringBuilder is mutable — append() modifies the internal buffer. Each append() returns the same StringBuilder (fluent API), enabling chaining. Return sb.toString() creates the final immutable String. Never use String + in loops — each + creates a new String object.

problems.title: Fill Array From Method
problems.description: Implement `fillFibonacci(int[] arr)` that fills the given array with Fibonacci numbers starting from F(0). The method should work in-place (no return value). Prove it works by printing arr from the caller.
problems.hint1: arr[0]=0, arr[1]=1, then arr[i] = arr[i-1] + arr[i-2].
problems.hint2: The array size determines how many Fibonacci numbers to generate.
problems.hint3: No return value needed — mutations to arr[i] are visible to caller.
problems.editorial: Fill in-place using the relation F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2). The method has no return value because it mutates through the reference — the caller's array is the same heap object.

problems.title: Accumulate Strings Into StringBuilder Parameter
problems.description: Implement `appendWords(StringBuilder sb, String[] words, String separator)` that appends all words to sb joined by separator. The caller should see the updated StringBuilder after the call. Verify this works and explain why it does.
problems.hint1: StringBuilder is mutable — sb.append() modifies the real object.
problems.hint2: Use a loop; skip separator after the last word.
problems.hint3: No return needed — the caller's sb variable points to the same object you're mutating.
problems.editorial: sb.append() calls on the parameter work because sb is a copy of the reference pointing to the SAME StringBuilder heap object. Mutations go through the reference and affect the real object. The method is effectively 'consuming' the caller's StringBuilder — a common Java pattern for building output in recursive algorithms.

problems.title: Clone vs Shallow Copy — Reference Trap
problems.description: Implement `shallowCopy(int[][] matrix)` that returns a new int[][] where the outer array is a copy but inner arrays are shared. Then `deepCopy(int[][] matrix)` where both outer and inner arrays are independent copies. Demonstrate the difference.
problems.hint1: Shallow: new int[matrix.length][], then copy references: copy[i] = matrix[i].
problems.hint2: Deep: new int[matrix.length][], then for each row: copy[i] = Arrays.copyOf(matrix[i], matrix[i].length).
problems.hint3: To prove shallow copy shares inner arrays: modify shallowCopy[0][0] and check if original[0][0] also changed.
problems.constraints: matrix is non-null, non-jagged (all rows same length).
problems.editorial: Shallow copy creates a new outer array but copies the row references — both outer arrays share the same inner arrays. Deep copy creates new inner arrays too — fully independent. This is the source of many bugs when 'copying' 2D arrays in Java.

problems.title: Accumulate Sum Using Counter Object
problems.description: Create a mutable `Counter` class with a `value` field. Implement `sumIntoCounter(Counter c, int[] nums)` that adds all nums to c.value in-place. Demonstrate that the caller sees the updated count. Then compare with trying to do the same with a plain int parameter.
problems.hint1: Counter is an object — its 'value' field can be mutated through the reference.
problems.hint2: c.value += nums[i] navigates through the reference to the real Counter object.
problems.hint3: A plain int parameter cannot be modified — you'd need to return it.
problems.editorial: c.value += n navigates through c (a copied reference) to the real Counter object on the heap and modifies its field. This is a real mutation — visible to caller. This pattern appears in recursive tree algorithms: pass a Counter and increment it at each node.

problems.title: Matrix Fill In-Place
problems.description: Implement `fillMultiplicationTable(int[][] matrix)` that fills matrix[i][j] = (i+1)*(j+1) in-place for a given n×n matrix. The caller should see the filled matrix after the call.
problems.hint1: matrix[i][j] = (i+1)*(j+1) for 0-indexed i and j.
problems.hint2: Two nested loops: outer for rows, inner for columns.
problems.hint3: No return needed — matrix is a reference to the heap array.
problems.editorial: matrix is a reference to the 2D array on the heap. matrix[i][j] = ... navigates through two references (outer array → row array → element) and modifies in-place. The caller's matrix variable still points to the same object and sees all changes.

problems.title: Implement Pair Swap Using Return
problems.description: Since Java can't swap primitives via method parameters, implement `swapPair(int a, int b)` that returns an int[] of {b, a}. Then use it to swap two variables in main. This is the idiomatic Java way to 'return' multiple values.
problems.hint1: return new int[]{b, a}; — simply reverse the two values.
problems.hint2: In the caller: int[] swapped = swapPair(x, y); x = swapped[0]; y = swapped[1];
problems.hint3: Alternatively, use Java 14+ record: record Pair(int first, int second) {}
problems.constraints: a and b fit in int.
problems.editorial: Return the swapped pair as int[]{b, a}. The caller unpacks it manually. This is the Java equivalent of multiple return values in Python/Go. Records (Java 14+) give names to the returned values: Pair(first=b, second=a) is self-documenting.

problems.title: Deep vs Shallow — Object List Copy
problems.description: Given a `Person` class with a mutable `name` field, implement `shallowCopyList(List<Person>)` and `deepCopyList(List<Person>)`. Demonstrate that modifying a Person in the shallow copy affects the original, but not in the deep copy.
problems.hint1: Shallow: new ArrayList<>(original) — new list, same Person references.
problems.hint2: Deep: for each Person, create a new Person with the same name: new Person(p.name).
problems.hint3: After copying, set shallowCopy.get(0).name = 'X' and see original.get(0).name change too.

testCases.input: deep copy mutation does not affect original

problems.editorial: new ArrayList<>(list) creates a new ArrayList but copies the Person references — shallow copy. Mutating p.name through a shallow copy reference changes the original. Deep copy creates new Person objects — fully independent. This distinction is critical for defensive programming and immutability.

problems.title: Tree Node Sum — Accumulate via Object
problems.description: Given a binary tree, implement `sumTree(TreeNode root, int[] total)` that accumulates the sum of all node values into total[0] using DFS. The int[] wrapper is needed because we can't increment a primitive across recursive calls.
problems.hint1: Base case: root == null → return.
problems.hint2: total[0] += root.val — mutates the int[] array element in place.
problems.hint3: Recurse into left and right children, passing the same total[] array.
problems.constraints: 1 <= nodes <= 10^4. Node values fit in int.
problems.editorial: total[0] += root.val navigates through the int[] reference to the actual element. All recursive calls share the SAME int[] object on the heap — mutations accumulate correctly. Alternative: return int from the recursive method and sum up (cleaner). int[] workaround is needed in lambdas and anonymous classes where you can't return values.

problems.title: Rotate Array In-Place
problems.description: Implement `rotate(int[] nums, int k)` that rotates the array to the right by k steps in-place. Use the reversal algorithm: reverse all, reverse first k, reverse rest. Verify the caller sees the result.
problems.hint1: k = k % nums.length to handle k >= nums.length.
problems.hint2: Reversal algorithm: reverse(nums, 0, n-1), reverse(nums, 0, k-1), reverse(nums, k, n-1).
problems.hint3: Implement a helper reverse(int[] arr, int lo, int hi).
problems.editorial: Reversal trick: reverse all (7654321), reverse first k=3 (5674321 → 5671234 → actually 5671234)... More precisely: [1234567] → reverse all → [7654321] → reverse [0,k-1]=[0,2] → [5674321] → reverse [k,n-1]=[3,6] → [5671234]. In-place, O(n) time, O(1) space. Classic LeetCode #189.

problems.title: Longest Common Prefix — Mutating Result Holder
problems.description: Find the longest common prefix string among an array of strings. Implement using a String[] result holder passed to a recursive helper. The helper should update result[0] as it narrows down the prefix.
problems.hint1: Start with result[0] = strs[0] (the full first string as initial prefix).
problems.hint2: For each string, narrow result[0]: while (!strs[i].startsWith(result[0])) result[0] = result[0].substring(0, result[0].length()-1);
problems.hint3: If result[0] becomes empty, stop early.
problems.editorial: result[0] is a String reference inside a String[] holder. Each recursive call can reassign result[0] to a shorter prefix — this change is visible to all calls because they all hold a reference to the same String[] object. Pure recursion without a holder would require returning the prefix at each level.

problems.title: Graph DFS — Visited Array Mutation
problems.description: Implement DFS on an adjacency list graph using a boolean[] visited array passed to the recursive DFS helper. Count the number of nodes visited starting from node 0. The visited[] array is shared across all recursive calls.
problems.hint1: visited[node] = true before recursing into neighbors.
problems.hint2: Skip already-visited nodes: if (visited[neighbor]) continue;
problems.hint3: The count can be tracked via int[] count = {0} passed alongside visited.
problems.editorial: visited[] and count[] are arrays on the heap, shared across all recursive DFS calls. visited[node] = true marks the node across all frames. count[0]++ accumulates the count. Both mutations are visible globally because all frames hold references to the same heap arrays.

problems.title: Flatten Nested List Into Result List
problems.description: Given a nested structure of integers (an Object that is either an Integer or a List<Object>), implement `flatten(Object input, List<Integer> result)` that recursively flattens all integers into result. The List is mutated in place.
problems.hint1: If input is Integer, add it to result directly.
problems.hint2: If input is List, iterate and recurse on each element.
problems.hint3: Use instanceof: if (input instanceof Integer i) { result.add(i); }
problems.constraints: Nesting depth <= 10. Values fit in int.
problems.editorial: result is a List<Integer> on the heap, shared across all recursive calls. result.add(i) mutates the same list — all levels of recursion see each other's additions. Pattern matching instanceof (Java 16+) makes the type check clean.

problems.title: Implement Object Pool Using Reference Semantics
problems.description: Implement a simple `ObjectPool<T>` with `acquire()` and `release(T obj)` methods. The pool holds reusable objects. Demonstrate that acquiring the same object and modifying it through two different 'variables' reflects on both — because they hold the same reference.
problems.hint1: Pool stores a Queue<T> of available objects.
problems.hint2: acquire(): return pool.poll() or null if empty.
problems.hint3: release(obj): pool.offer(obj) — returns the object for reuse.
problems.editorial: The pool stores references to objects. acquire() returns a reference to the same heap object. a.append('Hello') mutates it. release(a) puts the reference back in the queue. b = pool.acquire() gives you that same reference — b.toString() shows 'Hello' because a and b pointed to the same object. Real object pools (like database connection pools, Netty ByteBuf pool) work exactly this way.
